title: AI Assistance in Legal Analysis: An Empirical Study
venue: Journal of Legal Education, Volume 73, Number 2

study_design: We conducted our study using real examinations from two classes at the University of Minnesota Law School taught by one of the co-authors.
To evaluate the effect of access to AI on student exam performance, we recruited students who were taking either of these classes in the spring of 2023.
To participate in the study, students first completed a one-hour online training course that we developed and taught on how to use GPT-4 effectively in legal analysis.
After their training and review, study participants used GPT-4 to help them take a portion of the real exams that were administered in the same classes in the prior year (spring 2022).
Exams produced by AI-assisted humans and by AIs alone were then mixed with exams written by real students in 2022 without AI assistance, and blindly graded.
Blind grading helped to ensure that our preconceptions would not affect the results of the study.
This experiment received approval from the University of Minnesota's institutional review board. IRB #STUDY00019012.
task: The two courses we studied were Introduction to American Law and Legal Reasoning, and Insurance Law.

Introduction to American Law and Legal Reasoning is an introductory class offered to undergraduates at the University of Minnesota, covering selected topics in contract law, tort law, criminal law, civil procedure, property law and constitutional law.
Insurance Law is a more typical law school class that is taught principally to upper-level 2L and 3L University of Minnesota Law students.

These exams typically include both multiple-choice questions and essay questions that require students to analyze a novel fact pattern by clearly stating the relevant legal issue, specifying the applicable legal rules, applying those rules to the facts, analogizing and/or distinguishing to relevant case law, and considering key counterarguments.
Insurance Law grades are based principally on a single final exam, which consists of between two and four essay questions.
For Insurance Law, study participants answered two of the three questions from the spring 2022 Insurance Law final exam, each of which counted toward 25% of students' final grades in 2022.

For students in Introduction to American Law, we used fifteen multiple-choice questions from the 2022 final exam question and an essay question from the third 2022 exam, which covered property and civil procedure.
population: Sixteen out of fifty-two students taking Insurance Law (roughly 31%) completed the study, while thirty-two out of 128 students taking Introduction to American Law (roughly 23%) completed the study.
The University of Minnesota Law School is one of the nation's highly regarded public law schools.

Master's students and undergraduates who complete Law 3000 are also able to take Insurance Law with the instructor's permission. One study participant for Insurance Law was an undergraduate, though the rest were upper-level law students.

Attrition between recruitment and experiment completion was relatively low for both classes. In Insurance Law, eighteen students responded to recruiting e-mails, and sixteen in fact completed the study. For Introduction to American Law and Legal Reasoning, thirty-nine students indicated they would be part of the study, and thirty-two students ultimately completed the study.
We paid each participant a flat fee for their participation, unrelated to their exam performance and conditional only on successful completion of the trainings and the exam.
Participants agreed to take part in the study before receiving their final grades for the relevant courses.
arms: We recruited students from two different law school classes to participate in an experiment.

By comparing their performance on this second set of exams (in which they had access to GPT-4 and training on how to use it) with their performance on real exams (in which access to GPT-4 was banned), we explore how access to AI impacts human performance in professional work and educational settings generally and in legal contexts in particular.

In addition to producing AI-assisted human exams, we also generated four exams that were produced by AI alone. Each of these four AI-only exams was produced using one of four distinct prompting methods.
We evaluated the performance of basic prompting, "chain-of-thought" prompting, "few-shot" prompting, and "grounded" prompting.
outcome_definitions: We first report the relative performance of AI-assisted humans in terms of their percentile performance relative to the human exam-takers who did not have access to AI.
All results are stated in percentile points relative to a baseline of performance set by humans who took the exam without access to AI.
The percentiles in the "Human-Only Mean" row reflect the mean scores for the subset of students who volunteered for our study relative to all students enrolled in each of the studied classes.

For the "AI-Assisted Mean" row, the percentiles were calculated by evaluating how the exams produced by students given access to AI would have scored relative to all the students who completed this exam in 2022 without access to (and before the creation of) ChatGPT.
In addition to comparing humans, AI, and AI-assisted humans in percentile terms, we can construct letter grades for each of the exams based on our findings.
We examined how access to GPT-4 impacted the speed with which our participants completed their exams.
statistical_results: For multiple-choice questions, it dramatically improved student results, producing a twenty-nine percentile-point improvement relative to these students' performance on the real exam.
However, AI assistance had no effect on student grades in the essay components of the two exams.
The worst-performing students benefited enormously from AI, with gains of approximately forty-five percentile points.
By contrast, the best-performing students received worse grades when given access to AI, experiencing declines of approximately twenty percentile points.

In a more basic law school class introducing undergraduates to legal analysis, AI alone achieved grades between B+ and A relative to a B+ median, representing performance at median or toward the top of the class.
On an upper-level class covering advanced legal concepts, AI alone achieved grades between B and A– relative to a B+ median, representing solid performance but not at the top of the class.
Table 1 shows the relative performance of humans alone, AI alone, and AI-assisted humans on the various exam components.
For the Introduction to American Law multiple-choice questions, the Human-Only Mean was 59.1 (49.9, 67.3), the AI-Assisted Mean was 88.0 (81.0, 92.8), and the Mean Change was +28.9 (+20.3, +38.4).
For the Introduction to American Law essay question, the Human-Only Mean was 49.5 (39.1, 59.2), the AI-Assisted Mean was 53.0 (43.3, 62.1), and the Mean Change was +3.5 (–6.7, +15.3).
For Insurance Law, the Human-Only Mean was 47.5 (33.4, 63.3), the AI-Assisted Mean was 46.3 (32.2, 58.1), and the Mean Change was -1.3 (–9.7, +7.7).

For the Introduction to American Law multiple-choice questions, the Human-Only Standard Deviation was 25.8 (21.4, 31.7), the AI-Assisted Standard Deviation was 16.9 (12.5, 20.9), and the Standard Deviation Change was -8.9 (–16.1, -2.7).

For the Introduction to American Law essay question, the Human-Only Standard Deviation was 29.4 (25.1, 34.9), the AI-Assisted Standard Deviation was 28.0 (23.8, 32.8), and the Standard Deviation Change was -1.4 (–8.2, +5.2).
For Insurance Law, the Human-Only Standard Deviation was 31.6 (25.2, 39.6), the AI-Assisted Standard Deviation was 27.2 (21.9, 33.8), and the Standard Deviation Change was -4.4 (–12.4, +3.0).
Table 2 shows the performance of GPT-4 alone on each of the exam components, using four different prompting techniques.
For the multiple-choice questions in the Introduction to American Law exam, GPT-4 alone consistently performed well above the median human test-taker.
With few-shot and grounded prompting it received a perfect score.
For the multiple-choice questions in the Introduction to American Law exam, GPT-4 achieved 79 with basic prompting, 79 with chain of thought, 100 with few shot, and 100 with grounded prompting.
For the Introduction to American Law essay, GPT-4 achieved 60 with basic prompting, 56 with chain of thought, 56 with few shot, and 93 with grounded prompting.
For Insurance Law, GPT-4 achieved 5 with basic prompting, 15 with chain of thought, 30 with few shot, and 65 with grounded prompting.
Overall, GPT-4 performed significantly better on the relatively straightforward Introduction to American Law essay than it did on the much more complex and specialized Insurance Law essays.

With respect to the former, all four prompting strategies resulted in exams that scored above the class mean, and the best AI model—which, recall, grounded its answer in the relevant instructor lecture notes for the class—would have performed near the top of the Introduction to American Law class.
For Insurance Law, by contrast, basic prompting produced an exam toward the bottom of the grade distribution.

Notably, however, improved prompting strategies generated progressively better results in Insurance Law, with grounded prompting allowing GPT-4 to produce an exam that would have performed moderately above average in the class.
In Introduction to American Law, the median grade received by students in our study without AI assistance was a B.
With AI assistance, the median grade improved to an A–, a result that was driven by the improvement in students' multiple-choice answers, which counted for 50% of the overall exam score.
In Insurance Law, the median grade received by students in our study without AI assistance was a B/B+.
Applying this grading curve to the exam taken with AI assistance, that median grade was unchanged.
On its own in Introduction to American Law, GPT-4 attained an A– grade with basic prompting, a B+ grade with chain-of-thought prompting, and an A grade with both few-shot and grounded prompting.
This performance was relative to a whole-class median grade of B+ in Introduction to American Law, suggesting that GPT-4 systematically outperformed real students in that class.
In Insurance Law, GPT-4 achieved a B grade with basic prompting, a B/B+ grade with chain-of-thought prompting, a B+ grade with few-shot prompting, and an A– grade with grounded prompting.
This was also relative to a whole-class median grade of B+, suggesting that GPT-4's performance fluctuated around median depending on prompting techniques.
On the real exam without AI assistance, our participants took an average of 74.5 minutes out of 75 allotted minutes to finish the final exam for Introduction to American Law.
With access to GPT-4, our participants took an average of 62.9 minutes to finish the exam.

We were not able to track how long study participants took to complete their actual final exam in Insurance Law, but with access to GPT-4, they took an average of 115.4 minutes out of the time limit of 120 minutes.
Three participants completed the AI-assisted Introduction to American Law exam very quickly, in less than 25 minutes (out of the 75 minutes allotted).

When we exclude these three participants, the average time taken on the AI-assisted exam increases to 66.9 minutes, and the average time taken on the real human-only exam decreases to 73.1 minutes, still a statistically significant difference.
The correlation between grades assigned on these exams in 2022 (as part of the real classes) and in 2023 (as part of our experiment) for Introduction to American Law was 0.77, moderately high.
For Insurance Law, this correlation was much higher, at 0.94.
The correction factor for Introduction to American Law essays was –1.42 points out of 53 points, and for Insurance Law it was +1.375 points out of 50 points.
ambiguity_or_caveats: Our work represents an early attempt to gauge how student performance on exams improves with access to AI.
There are several reasons to be cautious about the external validity of this study.
First, the training we provided to students may have been inadequate.

As students increasingly use ChatGPT and similar technologies in their everyday lives, and as universities begin explicitly to instruct students to use AI models more effectively, students' performance improvement from access to AI may increase.
This is an inherent limitation of the sort of study we conducted, which can gauge the impact of AI in only a single setting.

Second, students may have been less motivated to exert maximum effort in our study than they were in their real graded exam, causing them to underperform and causing us to underestimate the benefit of access to AI.

Conversely, students might have been better prepared, more familiar with the format of the exam, or more at ease during our study than during their real exam, which would cause them to overperform and therefore cause us to overestimate the benefit of access to AI.
One piece of evidence suggesting that effort was not the main driver of our results was the amount of time taken in the actual exam versus the exam in our study.
In Insurance Law, our study participants took almost all of the time allotted to them, but their performance still did not improve on average.

In Introduction to American Law, the participants did take systematically less time on our study exam than on the real exam, but their performance did systematically improve on the multiple-choice component while remaining roughly unchanged on average in the essay component.
This suggests that the type of exam question was the primary determinant of the benefit of AI assistance, rather than effort as proxied by time taken.
Third, we should be cautious about extrapolating the results of our study to other settings, particularly nonlegal settings.
Law school exams may differ systematically from exams in other subjects, and law school exams may not accurately test the kind of skills that many lawyers use in real life.
We are currently working on a follow-on study that tests performance improvements on more realistic lawyering tasks when given access to AI.
Fourth and finally, the methodologies we have labeled "AI-only" vary in the extent to which they actually require some human input.
Although we did not edit or review the responses produced by GPT-4, generating the few-shot prompts required access to model exams and the selection of appropriate source materials.
If the AI model were asked to select its own model answers and source materials, its performance would likely have been worse.
Thus the performance of these models might be regarded as a ceiling on what is possible with current technology.

Note that the results in Figure 4 may largely be driven by mean reversion—that is, if student exam results are randomly drawn from some distribution, we would expect that students who had underperformed in one sitting of the exam would outperform in the next sitting relative to their prior sitting.
These results are therefore most meaningful to the extent that student performance on exams is consistent between sittings.

In theory, we could either parametrically or nonparametrically (based on the empirical distribution of exam scores in our sample) estimate both a baseline of the expected amount of mean reversion between exam sittings and the degree to which our results differed from this baseline.

However, constructing the baseline would require prior probabilities of the parallel forms reliability (also known as equivalent forms reliability) of law school exams, meaning the correlation between a student's scores in different sittings of the same exam type with different specific questions.
To our knowledge, no empirical research on the parallel forms reliability of law school exams currently exists.

We therefore present Figure 4 as is, noting that if one interprets Figure 4 to say that we can reject the null hypothesis for certain levels of human-only performance that access to AI does not affect performance, this interpretation crucially assumes perfect parallel forms reliability.

This discrepancy was likely attributable to the fact that a different subset of teaching assistants graded the real Introduction to American Law exams in 2022 than did the research assistants who regraded them as part of our experiment.
By contrast, all of the Insurance Law exams in both 2022 and 2023 were graded by the same professor, who teaches the class.
The students who volunteered for our study were not a representative sample across performance levels.
If they were, the curves for human-only performance (representing how well our participants did in percentile terms compared with the class as a whole) would have been roughly flat.
Instead, they were inverse-U shaped, suggesting that our sample underrepresented both low-performing and high-performing students.
Thus, average treatment effects in our study are most representative of the effect on roughly average students.
Memory decay is a cognitive process by which information stored in the brain is lost over time because of a lack of use or retrieval.
Although the precise speed of memory decay can vary greatly over time, research suggests that even a spaced review of the underlying material can significantly counteract memory decay.
We required all study participants to complete the experiment within two weeks after they finished their actual final exam in their classes, to minimize memory decay of the course material.
These exams were not publicly available and had not been previously provided to students in the spring 2023 versions of their classes.
The exams tested different specific material than that covered on the students' real 2023 exams.
However, the tested material was covered in the 2023 classes at a comparable level of depth as the material tested on the real 2023 exam.

The substantive material covered in both Introduction to American Law and Insurance Law was virtually identical in the spring of 2023 and the spring of 2022, when the co-author instructor taught these same classes.

notes: This is an empirical study with two main experiments: (1) a within-subjects comparison of law students' exam performance with and without access to GPT-4, and (2) an evaluation of GPT-4's performance alone using different prompting strategies. The study involved 16 Insurance Law students and 32 Introduction to American Law students. The paper reports results separately for multiple-choice and essay components of exams. A related randomized controlled trial is mentioned in footnote 12 (Choi, Monahan & Schwarcz, 2024, Lawyering in the Age of Artificial Intelligence, 109 Minn. L. Rev. 147).
